label: Epicurean atomism

scope.school: The Garden of Epicurus, with the Latin exposition of Lucretius
scope.period: c. 307 BCE (founding of the Garden) to the 1st century BCE (Lucretius)
limitations: Draft awaiting human domain review (D2-001); review needed before any scholarly authority is claimed.
Sourced from encyclopedic treatments of Epicurus; Lucretian elaborations and later Epicurean school history are only indirectly covered.

No claims are recorded for human_place or time as separate primitives: Epicurean texts treat humanity's standing through the mortality of the soul and the non-intervention of the gods, and time receives no distinct doctrinal treatment in the sources used. Absence means unspecified, not denial.

claims.claim: Only atoms and void are fundamentally real: 'only bodies and void exist per se, that is, exist without depending for their existence on something else'.

The elementary constituents of nature are 'undifferentiated matter, in the form of discrete, solid and indivisible particles ("atoms") below the threshold of perception, plus empty space'; and 'things cannot arise out of nothing, since otherwise there would be no need of specific seeds for specific plants and animals'.

The universe 'contains everything, and so there is nothing outside it by which to conceive an edge. Hence, it is infinite', with infinitely many atoms and worlds: 'our particular cosmos... is only a temporary agglomeration of atoms, and it is only one of an infinite number of such cosmoi'.

Worlds and organisms arise from atomic motion without design: Epicurus 'tries to explain away this apparent purposiveness in nature in a proto-Darwinian way', and given infinite space 'we must expect there to be a plurality of worlds, some like ours, some — within limits — different'.

There is no divine governance — 'the gods... do not interest themselves in human affairs, since this would compromise their beatitude' — but atomic motion includes 'a certain randomness... that takes the form of a minute swerve in their forward course'.
claims.notes: The swerve doctrine is well attested, but its mechanism and its relation to free action are disputed: 'it is not entirely clear how the swerve operates', and how it explains free will is the subject of extensive scholarly debate.

claims.claim: The soul is itself atomic and mortal: 'the soul's texture is too delicate to exist independently of the body that contains it', so 'death is annihilation'.
There is no afterlife or posthumous judgment: 'there can be no punishment after death', and 'death, Epicurus insists, is nothing to us, since while we exist, our death is not'.
The core human problem is disturbance rooted in false fears of gods and death; 'if one can banish fear about the future... then one will attain tranquility (ataraxia), the most exalted state'.
The goal of life is 'happiness (eudaimonia)... freedom from bodily pain and mental disturbance', achieved through correct natural philosophy that removes groundless fears.
claims.notes: The distinction between katastematic (state) and kinetic pleasure carries this doctrine, and 'the precise nature of this distinction is debated' among scholars.

claims.claim: Knowledge of nature rests on the senses: 'sensations, together with the perception of pleasure and pain, are infallible criteria', and 'all of our knowledge ultimately comes from the senses... we can trust the senses, when properly used'.

distinguishing_claims: Atoms and void are the only per se existents; nothing comes from nothing
Infinitely many worlds form and dissolve without design or providence
The soul is atomic and mortal; death is annihilation and 'nothing to us'
The random atomic swerve breaks strict determinism
common_confusions: Epicureanism is not atheism: gods exist but are blissfully indifferent and do not intervene.
'Pleasure' as the goal means absence of pain and disturbance (ataraxia), not sensual indulgence.
Unlike modern scientific naturalism, Epicurean atomism is an ethically motivated physics aimed at dispelling fear, not an experimental research program.

sources.title: Epicurus (Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
sources.author: David Konstan
sources.passage: Epicurus held that the elementary constituents of nature are undifferentiated matter, in the form of discrete, solid and indivisible particles ('atoms') below the threshold of perception, plus empty space. [...] the universe — in Greek, the 'all' — contains everything, and so there is nothing outside it by which to conceive an edge. Hence, it is infinite. [...] a certain randomness in the motion of atoms, that takes the form of a minute swerve in their forward course. [...] the soul's texture is too delicate to exist independently of the body that contains it... From this it follows that there can be no punishment after death. [...] Death, Epicurus insists, is nothing to us, since while we exist, our death is not. [...] sensations, together with the perception of pleasure and pain, are infallible criteria. [...] the gods... do not interest themselves in human affairs, since this would compromise their beatitude. (First published 2005-01-10; substantive revision 2022-07-08.)

sources.title: Epicurus (Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
sources.author: Tim O'Keefe
sources.passage: Only bodies and void exist per se, that is, exist without depending for their existence on something else. [...] Our particular cosmos, however, is only a temporary agglomeration of atoms, and it is only one of an infinite number of such cosmoi. [...] Epicurus, however, following Empedocles, tries to explain away this apparent purposiveness in nature in a proto-Darwinian way. [...] One important result of Epicurus' philosophy of mind is that death is annihilation. [...] The gods have no concern for us. In fact, they are unaware of our existence, and live eternally in the intermundia. [...] If one can banish fear about the future... then one will attain tranquility (ataraxia), the most exalted state. [...] All of our knowledge ultimately comes from the senses, thinks Epicurus, and we can trust the senses, when properly used.

review.notes: Drafted for D2-001 batch 1. Source URLs fetched and passages captured on 2026-07-06. Review needed: no human domain review yet; no scholarly authority claimed.
